content.assessment: By design, this proposal leaves the selection age, track structure, admission exam, and free-choice guarantee entirely untouched, meaning every governance and selection mechanism identified by this lens (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01, F-live-hungarian_education_system-02, F-live-hungarian_education_system-03) remains fully unchanged. This maximizes administrative compatibility and avoids any conflict with the minister's declared non-negotiable position on free choice. However, from an institutional-fit perspective, this also means the proposal does nothing to interrupt the documented sorting dynamics: the composition gap between selective-track and vocational-school parental education (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04), the coaching-market advantage (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05), and Hungary's EU-topping segregation ranking (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06) all remain structurally intact regardless of compensatory funding. The ministry's own characterization of 6/8-grade advantage as reflecting family background rather than value-added learning (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08) suggests compensatory investment addresses downstream symptoms rather than the selection mechanism itself.
opportunities: Could complement, without conflicting with, the ministry's stated review-only stance on the exam system, since it operates through a separate, non-structural channel (F-live-hungarian_education_system-10)
strengths: Fully compatible with existing legal and political constraints, since it does not touch the statutorily protected free-choice right or the track structure (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03)
threats: The ministry's own diagnosis that 6/8-grade performance reflects favorable family background rather than value-added instruction (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08) implies that compensatory funding directed at feeder/disadvantaged schools may not close the gap if the core selection mechanism continues concentrating advantaged families in one track
weaknesses: Does not alter any of the selection, access, or governance mechanisms identified by the lens as generating segregation, leaving Hungary's EU-topping segregation ranking structurally unaddressed (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06)

The documented gap in mothers' tertiary education between selective-track and vocational-school populations would likely persist unchanged since the admission mechanism itself is untouched (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04)
